title: Code optimization: identifying and eliminating bottlenecks
outline: The concept of a bottleneck and the goals of code optimization.
Measurement before optimization: profiling and benchmarking.
Algorithmic optimization versus micro-optimization.
Memory, input/output and concurrency as sources of slowdown.
The discipline of optimization: rules, risks and trade-offs.
body: Code optimization is the process of improving a program so that it uses computing resources more efficiently while preserving its correct behaviour. The most common goals are to reduce execution time and to lower memory consumption, but optimization can also target network traffic, disk operations, battery usage on mobile devices, or the size of the compiled binary. Optimization is never an end in itself; it is justified only when the program does not meet its performance requirements.

A bottleneck is the part of a system that limits its overall performance, in the same way that the narrow neck of a bottle limits how fast liquid can pour out. In software, a small fraction of the code usually accounts for the largest share of the running time. This observation is expressed by the empirical Pareto principle, often stated as 'roughly 80 percent of the time is spent in 20 percent of the code'. The practical consequence is clear: effort must be concentrated on the few places that actually dominate the cost, not spread evenly across the whole program.

The first and most important rule of optimization is: measure before you change anything. Guessing where a program is slow is unreliable, because human intuition about performance is frequently wrong. Developers use two main tools for measurement. A profiler records how much time is spent in each function and how often each function is called, producing a breakdown such as a call graph or a flat time table. A benchmark repeatedly runs a specific operation and reports its average and worst-case duration, allowing precise comparison of two implementations.

Profilers come in two families. A sampling profiler periodically interrupts the program and records the current call stack; it is lightweight and gives a statistical picture with little overhead. An instrumenting profiler inserts measurement code around every function call; it is more precise but slows the program and can distort the very timings it measures. For a first investigation a sampling profiler is usually preferred because it disturbs the program least.

The most powerful form of optimization is algorithmic. Replacing an algorithm of complexity O(n squared) with one of complexity O(n log n) can turn a task that takes minutes into one that takes milliseconds as the data grows. For example, searching a sorted array with linear scan costs O(n), while binary search costs O(log n); looking items up in a hash table costs on average O(1) instead of O(n) for a list. No amount of low-level tuning of a quadratic algorithm can compete with choosing a better algorithm in the first place.

Choosing the right data structure is inseparable from choosing the right algorithm. If a program repeatedly checks whether an element is present in a collection, a hash set is far better than a list. If it constantly inserts and removes at both ends, a deque is appropriate. If it needs the smallest element at any moment, a heap (priority queue) is the correct choice. Many performance problems disappear once the data structure matches the access pattern of the program.

Below the algorithmic level lies micro-optimization: small changes such as moving an invariant computation out of a loop, caching a repeated calculation, avoiding unnecessary object creation, or replacing an expensive operation with a cheaper equivalent. A classic example is hoisting: computing the length of an array once before a loop instead of on every iteration. Micro-optimizations give modest gains and should be applied only after the algorithm and data structures are already sound.

Memory behaviour is a frequent and easily overlooked bottleneck. Modern processors are much faster than main memory, so they rely on small, fast caches. Code that accesses memory sequentially benefits from good cache locality and runs quickly, while code that jumps randomly across memory suffers cache misses and stalls. Iterating over a contiguous array is therefore usually faster than following the scattered pointers of a linked list, even when their theoretical complexity is identical.

Excessive allocation and garbage collection are another source of slowdown in managed languages such as Java, C# and Python. Creating millions of short-lived temporary objects forces the runtime to spend time reclaiming them. Techniques such as reusing buffers, using object pools, and preferring value types where the language supports them reduce this pressure. In strings, repeatedly concatenating with the '+' operator inside a loop is a well-known trap; a string builder should be used instead.

Input and output operations are often the true bottleneck, because reading from a disk or a network is orders of magnitude slower than working in memory. Effective strategies include batching many small requests into one, buffering reads and writes, compressing data before transmission, and caching results that are expensive to obtain. A common database anti-pattern is the 'N+1 query problem', in which a program issues one query per item instead of a single query for all items; fixing it can improve response time dramatically.

Concurrency and parallelism can remove bottlenecks by using several processor cores at once, but they introduce their own hazards. Splitting work across threads only helps when the task can genuinely be divided and when the overhead of coordination does not exceed the gain. Amdahl's law formalises this: the maximum speed-up is limited by the fraction of the program that must run sequentially. A section protected by a lock that every thread must pass through can itself become the new bottleneck.

Compilers and runtimes already perform many optimizations automatically, such as inlining small functions, eliminating dead code, unrolling loops and, in just-in-time environments, recompiling hot code paths into optimized machine code. Programmers should understand these mechanisms so that they neither duplicate work the compiler already does nor write code in a way that prevents the compiler from optimizing it. Enabling the correct optimization level of the build is often the simplest useful step.

Optimization always involves trade-offs and risks. Faster code is frequently harder to read and maintain, and aggressive changes can introduce subtle bugs. Donald Knuth's famous warning that 'premature optimization is the root of all evil' means that clarity should come first, and tuning should follow only where measurement proves it necessary. Every optimization must be verified by re-running the tests to confirm that behaviour is unchanged and by re-running the benchmark to confirm that performance actually improved.

A disciplined optimization workflow can be summarised as a repeating cycle: define a measurable performance goal, profile to find the dominant bottleneck, form a hypothesis about its cause, make one focused change, and measure again to accept or reject that change. Working on one bottleneck at a time keeps cause and effect clear. Once the largest bottleneck is removed, a new one becomes dominant, and the process is repeated until the performance goal is met.

Thus, effective code optimization is a systematic, evidence-driven activity rather than a collection of tricks. It begins with measurement, concentrates on the small parts of the code that dominate the cost, prefers better algorithms and data structures over local hacks, and always verifies both correctness and the real performance gain, so that the program becomes faster without becoming unreliable or unreadable.
questions: What is a bottleneck, and why should optimization effort be concentrated on it rather than spread across the whole program?
Why must a developer measure a program with a profiler before optimizing it, and what is the difference between a sampling and an instrumenting profiler?
Why is algorithmic optimization usually more powerful than micro-optimization? Give an example.
How do memory cache locality and excessive object allocation affect the performance of a program?
What trade-offs and risks accompany optimization, and what does the warning against 'premature optimization' mean?

title: Testing and debugging: how to ensure the quality of program code
outline: The purpose of testing and the concept of software quality.
Levels of testing: unit, integration, system and acceptance.
Kinds of testing and the idea of test coverage.
The debugging process and its tools.
Automated testing, TDD and continuous integration.
body: Testing is the process of executing a program with the intention of finding defects and of confirming that it behaves as required. Debugging is the closely related activity of locating the cause of a defect that has already been detected and correcting it. Together they form the core of software quality assurance. It is important to understand that testing can demonstrate the presence of errors but can never prove their complete absence, as expressed in the well-known statement by Edsger Dijkstra.

The quality of program code is a multidimensional concept. Beyond simple correctness, it includes reliability, performance, security, usability and maintainability. A defect, often called a bug, is a flaw in the code that causes it to produce an incorrect or unexpected result. A useful distinction is made between an error (a mistake made by a person), the fault or defect it produces in the code, and the failure that the user observes when the faulty code runs.

Testing is organised into levels that correspond to the granularity of what is being tested. Unit testing checks the smallest testable pieces of code, such as individual functions or methods, in isolation. Integration testing checks that separately developed units work correctly together when combined. System testing exercises the complete, integrated application against its requirements. Acceptance testing, often performed with or by the customer, confirms that the system satisfies business needs and is ready for release.

A unit test typically follows the 'Arrange–Act–Assert' structure: it first prepares the inputs and the object under test, then invokes the operation being tested, and finally asserts that the actual result matches the expected result. Because a unit must be tested in isolation, its external dependencies are often replaced with test doubles such as stubs, which return canned answers, and mocks, which also verify that the unit interacts with them as expected.

Tests are also classified by what aspect they examine. Functional testing verifies what the system does against its specification. Non-functional testing examines qualities such as performance, load behaviour, security and usability. Regression testing re-runs existing tests after a change to make sure that previously working features have not been broken. Smoke testing runs a quick set of basic checks to decide whether a build is stable enough to justify deeper testing.

The design of good test cases relies on systematic techniques. Equivalence partitioning divides the possible inputs into groups that should be treated identically, so that one representative from each group is enough. Boundary value analysis focuses on the edges of these ranges, because errors cluster at boundaries such as zero, the maximum length, or the transition from one range to another. These techniques let a tester cover the important cases with a manageable number of tests.

Test coverage is a metric that measures how much of the code is exercised by the tests. Statement coverage counts the lines executed; branch coverage counts whether each direction of every decision has been taken. High coverage increases confidence, but it is not a guarantee of quality: code can be fully covered yet still contain logical errors, and chasing a coverage percentage for its own sake can produce weak tests that assert little. Coverage indicates what has not been tested more reliably than it certifies what has.

Debugging begins once a failure is observed. A reliable method is the scientific approach: reproduce the problem consistently, observe the symptoms, form a hypothesis about the cause, design an experiment to test that hypothesis, and confirm or reject it. Reproducing a bug reliably is often the hardest and most valuable step, because a defect that cannot be reproduced cannot be confidently fixed or verified.

Several tools and techniques support debugging. A debugger allows the developer to pause execution at breakpoints, step through the code line by line, and inspect the values of variables and the call stack. Logging records the program's behaviour over time and is especially valuable for problems that appear only in production. Assertions embedded in the code check assumptions and fail loudly when those assumptions are violated, catching defects close to their source.

A powerful debugging strategy for stubborn bugs is bisection: systematically narrowing the search space by half at each step. Applied to version history, the 'git bisect' command performs a binary search over commits to find the exact change that introduced a defect. The same divide-and-conquer idea applies within a single program, where the developer disables or isolates halves of the code to determine which part contains the fault.

Certain categories of bugs deserve special mention. A regression is a defect that reappears or is newly introduced in previously working functionality. A race condition is a timing-dependent bug in concurrent code that appears only under particular interleavings of threads and is notoriously hard to reproduce. A memory leak gradually consumes memory that is never released. Off-by-one errors, caused by an incorrect boundary such as using '<' instead of '<=', are among the most common and easily overlooked mistakes.

Modern practice relies heavily on automated testing so that the full test suite can be run frequently and cheaply. Test-driven development (TDD) inverts the usual order by writing a failing test first, then the minimum code to make it pass, and finally refactoring; this discipline keeps code testable and documents the intended behaviour. Continuous integration automatically builds the project and runs all tests whenever code is committed, catching defects within minutes of their introduction.

Not all defects can or should be fixed immediately, so teams manage them through a defect life cycle in a tracking system. A reported bug moves through states such as new, assigned, in progress, fixed, verified and closed, and is prioritised by its severity and by its impact on users. Good bug reports include the steps to reproduce, the expected result, the actual result and the environment, because a clearly described defect is far easier and cheaper to resolve.

It is far cheaper to find and fix a defect early than late. A mistake caught during coding costs little, while the same mistake discovered by a customer after release can be many times more expensive to correct and may damage reputation. This economic reality justifies investing in reviews, automated tests and continuous integration as preventive measures rather than treating testing as a final, optional stage.

Thus, ensuring the quality of program code is achieved not by a single activity but by a layered strategy: careful test design at several levels, meaningful use of coverage, a disciplined scientific approach to debugging supported by appropriate tools, and automation through TDD and continuous integration, all working together to detect defects as early and as cheaply as possible.
questions: Why can testing show the presence of defects but never prove their complete absence?
What are the four main levels of testing, and what does each of them check?
What is test coverage, and why does high coverage not by itself guarantee a defect-free program?
Describe the scientific approach to debugging and explain why reliably reproducing a bug is so important.
What are test-driven development and continuous integration, and how do they help to ensure code quality?

title: Architectural patterns: using MVC, MVVM and others
outline: The idea of architectural patterns and separation of concerns.
The Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern.
The Model-View-Presenter (MVP) and Model-View-ViewModel (MVVM) patterns.
Layered, client-server and other structural approaches.
Choosing a pattern and the trade-offs involved.
body: An architectural pattern is a general, reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem in the overall structure of a software system. Unlike a design pattern, which addresses the arrangement of a few classes, an architectural pattern shapes the whole application: how it is divided into major parts, how those parts communicate, and where different kinds of responsibility live. Using established patterns lets a team benefit from proven experience and share a common vocabulary.

The central principle behind most architectural patterns is separation of concerns: each part of the system should be responsible for one well-defined aspect and should know as little as possible about the others. This separation makes an application easier to understand, to test, to modify and to reuse, because a change in one concern, such as the appearance of the interface, does not force changes in an unrelated concern, such as the business rules.

The Model-View-Controller pattern, introduced in the late 1970s for Smalltalk, divides an interactive application into three roles. The Model holds the data and the business logic and is independent of how it is displayed. The View is responsible for presenting the data to the user. The Controller receives user input, decides how to act on it, and updates the Model. This triad keeps the data, its presentation and the handling of input clearly separated.

In classic MVC the flow of control is well defined. The user interacts with the View, which forwards the input to the Controller. The Controller interprets the action and updates the Model. The Model, having changed, notifies the View so that it can refresh itself, often through the observer mechanism. Because the Model knows nothing about the View, the same data can be presented in several different Views simultaneously.

MVC has become the dominant pattern for web frameworks such as Ruby on Rails, Django, Laravel and ASP.NET MVC, although the web adaptation differs slightly from the original desktop version. In a typical web request the Controller receives the incoming HTTP request, asks the Model for the required data, and selects a View (a template) to render the response that is sent back to the browser. This clear division keeps routing, data access and page rendering in separate places.

The Model-View-Presenter pattern is an evolution of MVC designed to make the presentation logic easier to test. Here the View is kept as passive and 'dumb' as possible, merely displaying what it is told and forwarding events. The Presenter contains all the presentation logic, retrieves data from the Model and formats it explicitly for the View. Because the View is passive and communicates through an interface, the Presenter can be tested without any real user interface.

The Model-View-ViewModel pattern, popularised by Microsoft with WPF and now central to many modern client frameworks, introduces a ViewModel between the View and the Model. The ViewModel exposes the data and commands that the View needs, in a form convenient for display, but it holds no reference to the View itself. The defining feature of MVVM is data binding: the View is connected to the ViewModel declaratively, so that changes in one are automatically reflected in the other.

Data binding is what makes MVVM attractive. Instead of writing code to copy each value from the data into a control and back again, the developer declares that a control's property is bound to a ViewModel property. When the underlying data changes, the interface updates by itself, and when the user edits a field, the ViewModel is updated automatically. This greatly reduces repetitive 'glue' code and keeps the View almost free of logic, which is why MVVM underlies frameworks such as WPF, Xamarin, Angular and Vue in spirit.

The three patterns are best understood as points on the same spectrum, differing mainly in how the presentation layer relates to the interface. MVC uses an active Controller that mediates input; MVP uses a Presenter that drives a passive View through an interface, favouring testability; MVVM uses a ViewModel joined to the View by automatic binding, favouring rich, reactive interfaces. All three keep the Model independent of presentation, which is their shared strength.

Beyond these presentation patterns, larger systems use structural patterns that organise the entire application. The layered (n-tier) pattern stacks the system into horizontal layers, typically presentation, business logic and data access, where each layer may use only the layer directly below it. This is one of the most widely used enterprise architectures because it maps naturally onto teams and onto the flow of a request through the system.

Other important architectural patterns address different needs. The client-server pattern splits the system into service providers and consumers. Microservices decompose an application into small, independently deployable services that communicate over the network, trading operational complexity for flexibility and independent scaling. Event-driven architectures let components react to events published on a bus, decoupling producers from consumers. The choice among them depends on the scale and evolution expected of the system.

No pattern is free of cost. Introducing MVC, MVP or MVVM adds classes and indirection that can feel like overhead in a very small program, where the separation may not pay for itself. MVVM in particular can be difficult to debug because the binding happens implicitly, and misused it can push logic into the wrong place. The layered pattern can create a 'sinkhole' where requests pass through layers that add no value. A pattern should be adopted because it solves a real problem in the project, not merely because it is fashionable.

Choosing an architecture is therefore a matter of matching the pattern to the requirements: the size of the system, the number of different views of the same data, the importance of automated testing, the richness and interactivity of the user interface, and the experience of the team. A well-chosen architecture makes future change cheaper; a poorly chosen or over-engineered one adds cost without benefit. The decision should be made deliberately and documented so that it can be revisited as the system grows.

Thus, architectural patterns such as MVC, MVP and MVVM are structured ways of applying separation of concerns to the presentation of an application, while patterns such as layered, client-server, microservices and event-driven architectures organise the system as a whole; understanding their goals, mechanisms and trade-offs allows a developer to select a structure that keeps the software understandable, testable and adaptable throughout its life.
questions: What is an architectural pattern, and how does the principle of separation of concerns underlie such patterns?
Describe the three components of MVC and explain the flow of control between them.
How does MVVM differ from MVC, and what role does data binding play in it?
Compare MVC, MVP and MVVM in terms of how the presentation logic relates to the user interface.
What trade-offs must be considered when choosing an architectural pattern for a project?

title: Development of multithreaded applications and parallel programming
objective: To form practical skills in creating multithreaded programs, distributing work across threads, and protecting shared data so that parallel execution is both faster and correct.
theory: A process is a running program with its own isolated memory, while a thread is an independent path of execution within a process; all threads of one process share the same memory, which makes communication between them cheap but also creates the risk of interference. Concurrency means structuring a program as several tasks that make progress independently, whereas parallelism means actually executing several tasks at the same instant on several processor cores. Multithreading is the main mechanism by which both are achieved on a single machine.

When several threads read and write the same data without coordination, the result depends on the unpredictable order in which the operations interleave; this defect is called a race condition. The section of code that accesses shared data and must not be executed by more than one thread at a time is called a critical section. Synchronization primitives such as mutexes (locks), semaphores and atomic operations are used to protect critical sections and guarantee that shared data remains consistent.

Synchronization introduces its own dangers. A deadlock occurs when two or more threads each hold a lock and wait forever for a lock held by the other. Excessive or coarse locking serialises the program and destroys the benefit of parallelism, while too little locking leaves race conditions. Modern languages provide higher-level tools, such as thread pools, futures, and 'async/await', that let developers express concurrent work without managing threads by hand, reducing the chance of these errors.
materials: A computer with a multi-core processor.
A development environment for a language with thread support (for example Java, C#, Python or C++).
The standard concurrency library of the chosen language (threads, locks, thread pool or async framework).
A stopwatch or the language's timing functions for measuring execution time.
A worksheet for recording thread counts and measured run times.
procedure: Write and run a single-threaded program that performs a measurable amount of work, such as summing a large array or counting prime numbers in a range, and record its execution time as a baseline.
Split the work into equal parts and create several threads that each process one part, then combine their partial results; measure the execution time with two, four and more threads.
Deliberately introduce a shared counter updated by all threads without protection, observe the incorrect total caused by the race condition, and record what happens over repeated runs.
Protect the shared counter with a mutex or an atomic operation, confirm that the result is now always correct, and note any change in performance caused by the synchronization.
Replace manual thread creation with a thread pool or an async mechanism provided by the language, and compare the clarity of the code and the run time with the manual version.
Plot or tabulate execution time against the number of threads, identify the point at which adding threads stops helping, and relate the observation to Amdahl's law and to synchronization overhead.
tasks: Implement a parallel computation (for example a parallel sum or a parallel image or text processing task) and demonstrate a measurable speed-up over the single-threaded version.
Create a program that reproduces a race condition on shared data, then fix it using an appropriate synchronization primitive, and explain in the report why the fix works.

Construct a small example that can lead to a deadlock between two locks, describe the conditions that cause it, and rewrite the code to prevent the deadlock (for example by ordering the locks consistently).
conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has formed the skills of dividing a task across threads, measuring the speed-up from parallelism, and protecting shared data with synchronization while avoiding race conditions and deadlocks.
questions: What is the difference between a process and a thread, and between concurrency and parallelism?
What is a race condition, and how do a critical section and a mutex prevent it?
What is a deadlock, and what conditions are necessary for it to occur?
Why does adding more threads eventually stop improving performance, and how does Amdahl's law explain this?
What advantages do thread pools and async/await offer compared with creating and managing threads manually?

title: Developing code for processing and storing large volumes of data
outline: The characteristics of big data and why ordinary methods fail.
Storage: distributed file systems, NoSQL and data warehouses.
Processing models: batch, streaming and MapReduce.
Scaling, partitioning and fault tolerance.
Practical techniques for writing memory-efficient data code.
body: The term big data refers to data sets so large or so fast-moving that traditional tools and single-machine techniques can no longer store or process them efficiently. Its characteristics are often summarised by several 'V' words: volume (the sheer quantity of data), velocity (the speed at which it arrives), variety (the mixture of structured, semi-structured and unstructured formats) and veracity (the uncertainty about its quality). When any of these exceeds what one computer can comfortably handle, a different approach is required.

The first practical rule when data does not fit in memory is that the program must not try to load it all at once. Instead of reading an entire file into a list, the code should stream it, processing one record or one block at a time so that only a small window of data is in memory. In many languages this is expressed with iterators, generators or lazy sequences, which produce values on demand rather than materialising the whole collection.

When data outgrows a single disk, it is spread across many machines in a distributed storage system. The Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) and cloud object stores such as Amazon S3 split large files into blocks and store several copies of each block on different machines. This replication provides fault tolerance: if one machine fails, the data survives on the others. Storing data across many nodes also allows many machines to read different parts of it in parallel.

Relational databases, which enforce a fixed schema and strong consistency, are not always the best fit for big data, so a family of NoSQL databases has emerged. Key-value stores such as Redis offer very fast lookups by key; document stores such as MongoDB hold flexible JSON-like records; wide-column stores such as Cassandra handle enormous write volumes; and graph databases model highly connected data. Each sacrifices some generality to excel at a particular access pattern and scale.

The choice between relational and NoSQL systems is illuminated by the CAP theorem, which states that a distributed data store cannot simultaneously guarantee consistency, availability and tolerance of network partitions; in the presence of a partition it must sacrifice either consistency or availability. Many NoSQL systems therefore adopt 'eventual consistency', accepting that different copies of the data may briefly disagree in exchange for higher availability and scalability.

Two fundamentally different strategies exist for handling growth. Vertical scaling (scaling up) means buying a more powerful single machine, which is simple but has a hard physical and economic limit. Horizontal scaling (scaling out) means adding more ordinary machines and distributing the work among them; it is the foundation of big-data systems because it can, in principle, grow without bound, but it requires software designed to coordinate many nodes.

Horizontal scaling relies on partitioning, also called sharding, which divides a large data set into disjoint pieces placed on different machines according to a key, such as a hash of the user identifier. Well-chosen partitioning spreads both storage and load evenly, so that queries touch only the relevant shards. A poor partition key causes 'hot spots', where one machine receives most of the traffic and becomes a bottleneck while others sit idle.

The classic model for processing data in bulk is MapReduce, popularised by Google and implemented in Hadoop. It expresses a computation as two functions: a map function that transforms each input record independently into key-value pairs, and a reduce function that aggregates all values sharing the same key. Because the map step processes records independently, it parallelises naturally across the cluster, and the framework handles distribution, sorting and fault recovery automatically.

MapReduce writes intermediate results to disk between stages, which makes it slow for iterative algorithms. Newer engines such as Apache Spark keep data in memory across steps and represent computations as a graph of transformations, achieving much higher speed for many workloads. Spark also unifies batch processing, interactive queries and machine learning in one framework, which is why it has largely superseded raw MapReduce for new development.

Big-data processing follows two broad timing models. Batch processing collects data over a period and then processes the whole set at once, which is efficient for reports and historical analysis. Stream processing handles each event as it arrives, with systems such as Apache Kafka, Flink or Spark Streaming, enabling near-real-time reactions such as fraud detection or live dashboards. Many organisations combine both in a single architecture to serve historical and immediate needs together.

Storing data for analysis has its own patterns. A data warehouse holds cleaned, structured data organised for fast analytical queries, following an 'extract, transform, load' (ETL) process. A data lake stores raw data of all kinds cheaply for later use, following a 'load then transform' philosophy. Columnar storage formats such as Parquet, which store each column together, dramatically speed up analytical queries that read only a few columns from billions of rows.

Even without a cluster, a developer can process surprisingly large data on one machine by writing memory-conscious code. Reading files line by line, using compact binary formats, compressing data, processing in fixed-size chunks, and choosing efficient numeric types all reduce memory pressure. Libraries such as pandas allow chunked reading, and specialised structures such as probabilistic data structures (for example a Bloom filter) answer membership questions using a tiny fraction of the memory an exact structure would need.

Correctness and cost also matter at scale. Because computations run across many machines and can fail partway, big-data pipelines are designed to be idempotent and restartable, so that re-running a failed step does not corrupt results. Monitoring, data validation and clear handling of malformed or missing records are essential, because at billions of rows even a rare error occurs many times and can silently distort conclusions.

Thus, developing code for large volumes of data means abandoning the assumption that everything fits in memory on one machine, and instead adopting streaming processing, distributed and appropriately chosen storage, horizontal scaling through partitioning, and parallel processing models such as MapReduce and Spark, while writing memory-efficient, fault-tolerant code so that the system remains fast, reliable and correct as the data grows.
questions: What are the main characteristics (the 'V's) of big data, and why do they make single-machine methods insufficient?
Why should code stream large data instead of loading it entirely into memory, and how is this expressed in a program?
What is the difference between vertical and horizontal scaling, and what role does partitioning (sharding) play in the latter?
Explain the MapReduce model and why the map step parallelises so naturally across a cluster.
What is the difference between batch and stream processing, and give an example use case for each?

title: Using data structures: lists, arrays and hash tables
outline: What a data structure is and why the choice matters.
Arrays: contiguous memory and constant-time indexing.
Linked lists and dynamic lists.
Hash tables: hashing, collisions and average O(1) access.
Choosing the right structure for the access pattern.
body: A data structure is a particular way of organising and storing data in a computer's memory so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently. The choice of data structure is one of the most consequential decisions a programmer makes, because it determines how fast the common operations of a program will be. The same task can run in a fraction of a second or grind to a halt purely because of whether the data is held in a list, an array or a hash table.

To reason about efficiency, developers use Big-O notation, which describes how the running time or memory of an operation grows as the number of elements n increases. Constant time O(1) does not depend on n; linear time O(n) grows in proportion to n; logarithmic time O(log n) grows very slowly. When comparing data structures, the relevant question is the cost of each operation, such as access, search, insertion and deletion, in this notation.

An array is the most fundamental data structure: a block of elements stored in contiguous memory, one after another. Because every element occupies the same amount of space and they are laid out sequentially, the address of any element can be computed directly from its index by simple arithmetic. This gives arrays their defining strength: access by index in constant time O(1), regardless of how large the array is.

The contiguity of arrays also gives excellent cache performance, because reading one element brings its neighbours into the processor cache, making sequential traversal very fast. The price of this layout is rigidity: a classic static array has a fixed size, and inserting or deleting an element in the middle requires shifting all the following elements, an O(n) operation. Adding to the end is cheap, but adding elsewhere is expensive.

A dynamic array, such as a Python list, a Java ArrayList or a C++ vector, removes the fixed-size limitation. It keeps a larger underlying array than currently needed and, when it fills up, allocates a bigger one (typically double the size) and copies the elements across. Although an individual resize costs O(n), it happens rarely, so the average cost of appending an element remains constant; this is described as amortised O(1) time.

A linked list takes the opposite approach: each element, called a node, stores its value together with a pointer to the next node, and the nodes may sit anywhere in memory. Because there is no index arithmetic, reaching the k-th element requires walking the chain from the start, an O(n) operation, and searching is likewise O(n). Linked lists therefore give up fast random access.

In return, a linked list makes insertion and deletion cheap when the position is already known: it is enough to change a few pointers, an O(1) operation, with no shifting of other elements. A doubly linked list adds a pointer to the previous node as well, allowing traversal in both directions and easy removal of a node given only a reference to it. Linked lists also grow and shrink one node at a time without any resizing.

The comparison between arrays and linked lists is a classic illustration of trade-offs. Arrays win on indexed access and cache locality; linked lists win on cheap insertion and deletion in the middle when the location is known. In practice, contiguous arrays often outperform linked lists even where theory seems to favour the list, precisely because sequential memory access is so much faster on modern hardware than following scattered pointers.

A hash table (also called a hash map or dictionary) stores key-value pairs and is designed for one thing above all: extremely fast lookup by key. It works by passing the key through a hash function, which produces a number that is reduced to an index into an internal array of buckets. The value is stored in that bucket, so retrieving it later requires only recomputing the hash and going straight to the right bucket, giving average constant-time O(1) access, insertion and deletion.

The difficulty in a hash table is that two different keys can hash to the same bucket, an event called a collision. There are two main strategies for resolving collisions. In separate chaining, each bucket holds a small list of all entries that map to it. In open addressing, a colliding entry is placed in another free bucket found by a probing sequence. Either way, the aim is to keep collisions rare so that operations stay close to O(1).

The performance of a hash table depends on its load factor, the ratio of stored entries to available buckets. As the table fills, collisions become more frequent and operations slow toward O(n) in the worst case. To prevent this, the table is rehashed when the load factor crosses a threshold: a larger bucket array is allocated and every entry is re-inserted. A good hash function that spreads keys uniformly is essential, and keys used in hash tables must be immutable so their hash does not change while stored.

These three structures are the foundation from which others are built. Stacks and queues are usually implemented on top of arrays or linked lists; sets and dictionaries are usually hash tables; and more advanced structures such as trees and graphs combine nodes and pointers in richer arrangements. Understanding the cost profile of the basic three is therefore the key to understanding almost every collection a language provides.

In everyday programming the practical guidance is straightforward. Use an array or dynamic list when the data is naturally ordered, accessed by position, or iterated sequentially. Use a linked list when frequent insertion and deletion at known positions dominate and indexed access is rare. Use a hash table whenever the primary operation is looking data up by a key or checking membership, because its average constant-time access is unmatched for that purpose.

Thus, lists, arrays and hash tables each embody a different trade-off between the costs of access, search, insertion and deletion; mastering their internal mechanics and their Big-O behaviour allows a developer to match the structure to the program's dominant access pattern, which is often the single most effective way to make a program both correct and fast.
questions: Why can an array access any element by its index in constant time O(1)?
What are the trade-offs between an array and a linked list for access, insertion and deletion?
How does a dynamic array achieve amortised O(1) appending despite occasional resizing?
How does a hash table achieve average O(1) lookup, and what is a collision?
What is the load factor of a hash table, and why does rehashing become necessary?

title: Creating and using objects and classes in object-oriented programming
outline: Classes and objects: the blueprint and its instances.
Fields, methods, constructors and the role of 'this'.
Encapsulation and access modifiers.
Inheritance, polymorphism and abstraction.
Object relationships and good class design.
body: Object-oriented programming (OOP) is a paradigm that organises software around objects, which bundle together data and the operations that act on that data. It arose to manage the complexity of large programs by modelling them as collections of interacting entities that resemble things in the problem domain, such as a customer, an account or a document. At the centre of the paradigm stand two closely related notions: the class and the object.

A class is a blueprint, or template, that defines the structure and behaviour common to a category of objects. An object is a concrete instance created from that blueprint, occupying its own memory and holding its own data. The relationship is like that between an architectural plan and the individual houses built from it: one class can produce many objects, each independent, yet all sharing the same defined structure and set of operations.

The data held by an object is stored in fields, also called attributes or member variables, and its behaviour is defined by methods, which are functions that belong to the class. Together the fields describe the state of an object at a given moment, and the methods describe what the object can do or what can be done to it. An object of a class BankAccount, for instance, might hold a balance field and offer deposit and withdraw methods that change it.

Objects are created through a special method called a constructor, which runs when an object is instantiated (in many languages using a keyword such as 'new') and sets the object's initial state. Within a method, the keyword 'this' (or 'self' in some languages) refers to the particular object on which the method was called, allowing the code to distinguish that object's own fields from parameters or other data. Some languages also provide a destructor or finalizer to release resources when an object is no longer needed.

The first pillar of OOP is encapsulation: bundling the data and the methods that operate on it inside the object, and hiding the internal details behind a controlled interface. Access modifiers such as private, protected and public express this. Fields are typically made private so they cannot be changed directly from outside, and access is provided through public methods (often called getters and setters) that can validate changes and preserve the object's invariants.

Encapsulation matters because it protects an object from being put into an invalid state and because it lets the internal implementation change without affecting the rest of the program, as long as the public interface stays the same. For example, a Temperature class might store its value internally in Celsius but expose methods to read it in either Celsius or Fahrenheit; callers depend only on the methods, not on how the value is actually stored.

The second pillar is inheritance, which lets a new class, the subclass or derived class, reuse and extend the fields and methods of an existing class, the superclass or base class. This models an 'is-a' relationship: a SavingsAccount is a BankAccount, so it inherits the balance and the deposit method while adding its own interest logic. Inheritance promotes reuse but must be used carefully, because it tightly couples the subclass to its parent.

A subclass can override an inherited method, providing its own version that replaces the parent's behaviour for objects of that subclass. This leads directly to the third pillar, polymorphism, which literally means 'many forms'. Polymorphism allows code written in terms of a general type to work with objects of many specific subtypes, each responding to the same method call in its own way; a program can hold a list of Shape objects and call area() on each, and every shape computes its area differently.

The mechanism that makes this work is dynamic dispatch: at run time the program selects the method implementation that matches the actual type of the object, not the type of the variable that refers to it. This is enormously powerful because it lets a system be extended with new subtypes without changing the code that uses them, as long as they honour the expected interface. Polymorphism is the main reason object-oriented code can be so flexible and extensible.

The fourth pillar is abstraction: exposing only the essential features of something while hiding the irrelevant detail. Abstract classes and interfaces express this. An interface defines a set of method signatures that an implementing class must provide but contains no implementation itself, acting as a contract. Programming to interfaces rather than to concrete classes lets different implementations be substituted freely, which is the foundation of loosely coupled, testable design.

Objects rarely exist in isolation; they are connected by relationships. Association is a general link in which one object uses another. Composition is a strong 'part-of' relationship in which one object owns another and controls its lifetime, such as a Car owning its Engine. Aggregation is a weaker whole-part relationship in which the parts can exist independently. A widely respected design guideline is to 'favour composition over inheritance', because composition is more flexible and avoids the fragility of deep inheritance hierarchies.

Good class design follows several principles. A class should have a single, well-defined responsibility rather than trying to do many unrelated things. It should expose a small, clear public interface and keep its internals private. Names should reflect the domain, and methods should be short and focused. These ideas are captured formally in the SOLID principles, whose first rule, the single-responsibility principle, states that a class should have only one reason to change.

Beginners commonly make several mistakes that undermine object orientation: creating 'god objects' that do everything, exposing fields publicly so any code can corrupt them, building deep inheritance chains that are hard to follow, and writing anaemic classes that hold data but contain no behaviour, leaving the logic scattered elsewhere. Awareness of these anti-patterns, together with the four pillars and the design principles, guides a developer toward classes that are cohesive and easy to maintain.

Thus, classes and objects provide the means to model a problem as self-contained entities that combine state and behaviour, while the pillars of encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism and abstraction, applied with sound design principles such as single responsibility and favouring composition, allow a developer to build large programs that are understandable, reusable, flexible and safe to change.
questions: What is the difference between a class and an object, and how are objects created from a class?
What is encapsulation, and how do access modifiers and getters/setters support it?
Explain inheritance and polymorphism, and describe how dynamic dispatch enables polymorphic behaviour.
What is the difference between composition and inheritance, and why is composition often preferred?
What is abstraction, and what is the purpose of an interface in object-oriented design?

title: Working with databases: SQL and ORM technologies
outline: Relational databases and the role of the DBMS.
The SQL language: DDL, DML and queries.
Joins, keys, indexes and transactions.
The object-relational impedance mismatch and ORM.
Benefits, pitfalls and choosing between SQL and ORM.
body: Most applications need to store data that outlives a single run of the program, and the dominant tool for this is the relational database, managed by a database management system (DBMS) such as PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle or SQL Server. In the relational model, data is organised into tables (relations), where each row represents one record and each column represents one attribute. This simple, tabular structure, combined with a powerful query language, has made relational databases the backbone of business software for decades.

The language used to work with relational databases is SQL, Structured Query Language. SQL is declarative: the programmer states what data is wanted rather than how to fetch it, and the database's query optimizer decides the most efficient way to obtain it. SQL is conventionally divided into sublanguages: the Data Definition Language (DDL) for defining structure, the Data Manipulation Language (DML) for changing data, and the query part for reading it.

DDL statements such as CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE and DROP TABLE define and modify the schema, specifying the columns, their data types and the constraints that keep the data valid. Constraints include NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK conditions, and above all the keys that give the relational model its integrity. Defining the schema carefully is important because the structure shapes everything the application can later do with the data.

The central query statement is SELECT, which retrieves rows from one or more tables. A SELECT lists the columns wanted, names the table in a FROM clause, filters rows with a WHERE clause, groups them with GROUP BY, aggregates them with functions such as COUNT, SUM and AVG, and orders the result with ORDER BY. DML statements INSERT, UPDATE and DELETE add, change and remove rows. Mastery of these statements is the core skill of working with any relational database.

Keys are what tie the model together. A primary key is a column (or set of columns) that uniquely identifies each row of a table. A foreign key is a column in one table that refers to the primary key of another, expressing a relationship between them, such as an order referring to the customer who placed it. Foreign keys enforce referential integrity, preventing, for instance, an order that points to a non-existent customer.

Because data is spread across related tables, queries frequently need to combine them, which is done with a JOIN. An inner join returns only rows that have matching values in both tables, while outer joins (left, right and full) also keep unmatched rows, filling the missing side with nulls. Understanding joins is essential, because normalised databases deliberately split information across many tables to avoid duplication, and joins reassemble it when needed.

Two further concepts are vital for correct and fast databases. An index is an auxiliary structure, usually a B-tree, that lets the database find rows matching a condition without scanning the whole table, turning a slow O(n) search into a fast O(log n) lookup; indexes speed up reads but slow down writes and consume space, so they are added deliberately. A transaction groups several operations so that they either all succeed or all fail together, guaranteeing the ACID properties of atomicity, consistency, isolation and durability, which is what allows a bank transfer to be safe even if the system crashes midway.

While relational databases think in tables and rows, object-oriented programs think in objects with fields, references and inheritance. These two worldviews do not line up neatly, a difficulty known as the object-relational impedance mismatch. Writing raw SQL inside an application also forces the developer to convert manually between query results and objects, to scatter SQL strings through the code, and to rewrite queries for each different database dialect.

Object-relational mapping (ORM) technology addresses this mismatch by automatically translating between database tables and program objects. An ORM lets the developer define classes that correspond to tables, so that saving an object writes a row and loading a row produces an object. Popular ORMs include Hibernate and JPA for Java, Entity Framework for .NET, the Django ORM and SQLAlchemy for Python, and Active Record for Ruby on Rails.

The benefits of an ORM are considerable. It lets developers work in the natural idiom of their programming language rather than composing SQL strings, it handles much of the repetitive conversion code, and it can insulate the application from the specific database product, easing a later switch. Because queries are built through the language's own type system, many mistakes are caught at compile time, and features such as change tracking and lazy loading simplify common tasks.

ORMs also carry well-known pitfalls. Because they hide the generated SQL, they can silently produce inefficient queries; the most infamous is the N+1 problem, where fetching a list and then its related data one item at a time issues one query per row instead of a single join. ORMs add a learning curve and a layer of abstraction that can obscure what the database is actually doing, and very complex or highly tuned queries are sometimes still best written by hand in SQL.

Security and correctness must be considered whichever approach is used. SQL injection, one of the most dangerous web vulnerabilities, occurs when user input is concatenated directly into a query, allowing an attacker to alter its meaning; it is prevented by using parameterised queries or prepared statements, which most ORMs apply automatically. Regardless of the tool, input must be validated and the principle of least privilege applied to database accounts.

In practice, SQL knowledge and ORM usage are complementary rather than competing. A capable developer uses the ORM for the routine majority of operations to gain productivity and safety, while retaining the ability to read the SQL it generates, to add indexes where profiling shows they are needed, and to drop down to hand-written SQL for the rare, performance-critical or complex queries. Understanding the database beneath the ORM is what separates effective use from blind reliance.

Thus, working with databases rests on a solid grasp of the relational model and SQL, including tables, keys, joins, indexes and transactions, complemented by ORM technologies that bridge the gap between objects and tables to raise productivity; using both wisely, while guarding against inefficient queries and injection attacks, lets an application store and retrieve data reliably, safely and efficiently.
questions: What is the relational model, and what roles do primary keys and foreign keys play in it?
What are the main parts of SQL, and what does a SELECT statement with WHERE, GROUP BY and ORDER BY do?
Why do indexes speed up queries, and what do the ACID properties of a transaction guarantee?
What is the object-relational impedance mismatch, and how does an ORM address it?
What are the main pitfalls of using an ORM, and how is SQL injection prevented?

title: Integration and interaction between software components
objective: To form practical skills in connecting separate software components through defined interfaces so that they exchange data correctly and function as a single integrated system.
theory: Modern software is built from separate components that must interact: modules within one program, libraries, and independent services communicating over a network. The contract that governs this interaction is the interface, or application programming interface (API), which specifies what operations a component offers, what data it expects, and what it returns, while hiding its internal implementation. Well-defined interfaces let components be developed, tested and replaced independently, provided the contract is respected.

Components communicate through several common mechanisms. A direct function or method call is used within a single process. Over a network, the dominant style today is the REST web API, in which a client sends HTTP requests (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to resource addresses and exchanges data, usually in JSON format. Other styles include remote procedure calls such as gRPC, and asynchronous messaging through a message queue or broker, where components communicate by publishing and consuming messages without waiting for each other.

Successful integration depends on agreement about the data format and on robust handling of failure. Both sides must serialise and deserialise data in the same agreed structure, commonly JSON or XML, and must agree on the meaning of status codes and error responses. Because a networked component can be slow, unavailable or return unexpected data, integration code must validate inputs and outputs, handle timeouts and errors gracefully, and never assume that a remote call will always succeed.
materials: A computer with a development environment for the chosen language.
An HTTP client tool for testing APIs (for example Postman, curl or a browser).
A library for making HTTP requests and for parsing JSON.
Access to a sample public REST API or a small local server component to integrate with.
A specification or documentation describing the interface of the component being used.
procedure: Study the documented interface of a provided component or public REST API, identifying its available operations, the required input parameters, and the structure of the data it returns.
Write a small client that calls one operation of the component, sends the correct request, receives the response, and parses the returned JSON into objects your program can use.
Extend the client to send data to the component (for example with a POST request), and verify from the response and status code that the data was accepted correctly.
Add error handling that detects timeouts, unsuccessful status codes and malformed responses, and reports a clear message instead of crashing.
Integrate two components so that the output of one becomes the input of another, and confirm that data flows correctly through the combined pipeline.
Test the integration with both valid and invalid inputs, record how each component behaves, and document the interface contract you relied upon.
tasks: Write a program that consumes a REST API, retrieves data from it, parses the JSON response, and displays selected fields to the user.
Implement robust error handling for the integration so that network failures, timeouts and invalid responses are caught and reported without crashing the program.
Combine two separate components (for example one that fetches data and one that processes or stores it) into a single working pipeline and describe the interface between them in the report.
conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has formed the skills of connecting software components through defined interfaces, exchanging and parsing data over an API, and handling integration failures so that the components operate reliably as one system.
questions: What is an API, and why is a well-defined interface essential for integrating software components?
What are the main mechanisms by which components communicate, and how does a REST API work?
Why are data serialization formats such as JSON important in component integration?
Why must integration code handle timeouts and errors, and what can happen if it does not?
What is the difference between synchronous calls and asynchronous messaging between components?

title: Developing web applications: client and server sides
outline: The client-server model and the request-response cycle of the web.
The front-end: HTML, CSS, JavaScript and the browser.
The back-end: server logic, application servers and databases.
Communication between client and server: HTTP, APIs and JSON.
Rendering strategies, security and the modern web stack.
body: A web application is a program that runs across two cooperating sides connected by a network: a client, running in the user's web browser, and a server, running on a remote machine. This division follows the client-server model, in which the client requests services and the server provides them. Understanding a web application means understanding what each side is responsible for and how they communicate, because a feature is almost always the result of the two sides working together.

The web is built on the HTTP protocol and its secure form HTTPS, which follow a request-response cycle. The browser sends an HTTP request identifying a resource by its URL and a method such as GET or POST; the server processes it and returns an HTTP response containing a status code, headers and a body. A defining property of HTTP is that it is stateless: each request is independent, and the server does not automatically remember previous requests, so state must be maintained deliberately.

The client side, also called the front-end, is everything that runs in the browser and is responsible for what the user sees and interacts with. It is built from three core technologies: HTML, which defines the structure and content of the page; CSS, which controls its appearance and layout; and JavaScript, which adds behaviour and interactivity. The browser parses the HTML into a tree called the Document Object Model (DOM), which JavaScript can read and modify to change the page dynamically.

Front-end development has grown far beyond simple pages. Frameworks and libraries such as React, Angular and Vue help developers build complex, interactive user interfaces by organising the code into reusable components and by efficiently updating only the parts of the DOM that change. These tools manage the application's state on the client and are the basis of the single-page application, in which the browser loads one page and then updates its content dynamically without full reloads.

The server side, or back-end, is the code that runs on the server and is responsible for the application's core logic, for enforcing business rules, for security, and for storing and retrieving data. It is written in server-side languages and frameworks such as Node.js with Express, Python with Django or Flask, Java with Spring, PHP with Laravel, or Ruby on Rails. The back-end receives requests, performs the required work, talks to the database, and produces the response.

The back-end almost always works with a database and other services. When a request arrives, the server may authenticate the user, query or update a database, call external APIs, apply business rules, and then assemble a result. Because the server is under the developer's control and hidden from the user, it is the correct place for anything sensitive or authoritative: passwords are checked here, prices are calculated here, and permissions are enforced here, never solely in the browser.

The two sides communicate through well-defined interfaces, most commonly a web API. In the widespread REST style, the server exposes resources at URLs, and the client uses HTTP methods to read and change them, exchanging data in JSON format, a lightweight text representation of structured data. The client sends a request to an endpoint such as '/api/users', the server responds with JSON, and the client's JavaScript turns that data into what the user sees.

There are two broad strategies for producing the HTML the user sees. In server-side rendering, the server generates the complete HTML page for each request and sends it ready-made; this is good for fast first loads and for search-engine visibility. In client-side rendering, the server sends a minimal page plus JavaScript, and the browser builds the interface and fetches data through APIs; this gives a smooth, app-like experience. Modern frameworks increasingly combine both to gain the advantages of each.

Because HTTP is stateless, web applications need a mechanism to remember who a user is across requests. This is done with sessions and cookies, or with tokens such as JSON Web Tokens. After a user logs in, the server issues an identifier that the browser returns with each subsequent request, allowing the server to recognise the user and maintain a logged-in state. Managing this authentication and authorization correctly is a central concern of web development.

Security is not optional in web applications, because they are exposed to the whole internet. Common threats are catalogued by the OWASP project and include SQL injection, in which malicious input alters a database query; cross-site scripting (XSS), in which injected script runs in another user's browser; and cross-site request forgery (CSRF), in which a user is tricked into making an unwanted request. Defences include validating and escaping all input, using parameterised queries, encoding output, using HTTPS everywhere, and never trusting data that comes from the client.

A running web application usually involves more than a single client and server. Requests may pass through a load balancer that spreads traffic across several server instances, a reverse proxy or web server such as Nginx, a caching layer such as Redis to avoid repeating expensive work, and a content delivery network that serves static files from locations close to the user. These pieces of infrastructure allow the application to remain fast and available as the number of users grows.

Developers commonly describe their choice of technologies as a 'stack'. A full-stack developer works on both the client and the server sides. Popular combinations include the MERN stack (MongoDB, Express, React, Node.js) and the traditional LAMP stack (Linux, Apache, MySQL, PHP). Whatever the specific choice, the fundamental separation of responsibilities between a presentation-focused client and a logic- and data-focused server remains the same.

Building a web application therefore requires coordinated attention to both sides: a front-end that is responsive, accessible and pleasant to use, and a back-end that is correct, secure and efficient, joined by a clear API contract. Testing must cover both sides and their interaction, and deployment must consider the surrounding infrastructure. The most common source of bugs is a mismatch between what the client sends and what the server expects, which underlines the importance of a precisely defined interface.

Thus, developing a web application means engineering two cooperating sides connected by the stateless HTTP protocol: a browser-based client built with HTML, CSS and JavaScript that presents the interface, and a server built with a back-end language and a database that holds the authoritative logic and data, communicating through a well-defined API and protected by deliberate security measures so that the whole system is fast, reliable and safe.
questions: What responsibilities belong to the client side and to the server side of a web application?
Describe the HTTP request-response cycle and explain what it means that HTTP is stateless.
What are the three core front-end technologies, and what is the role of the DOM?
How do the client and server typically communicate in a REST-based application, and why is JSON used?
Name three common web security threats and describe how each can be prevented.

title: Developing mobile applications: iOS, Android and cross-platform solutions
outline: What makes mobile development distinctive.
Native Android development.
Native iOS development.
Cross-platform frameworks and their approaches.
Choosing between native and cross-platform; distribution and constraints.
body: Mobile application development is the creation of software that runs on smartphones and tablets, and it differs in important ways from developing for desktops or servers. Mobile devices have limited battery, memory and processing power; they use touch as the primary input; their screens are small and vary greatly in size; and their network connectivity is intermittent. A mobile app must be designed with these constraints in mind, prioritising responsiveness, efficient use of resources and a touch-friendly interface.

The mobile market is dominated by two operating systems, Google's Android and Apple's iOS, which together cover almost all devices. Because they are separate platforms with different languages, tools and design conventions, a developer must decide whether to build separately for each (the native approach) or to write shared code that runs on both (the cross-platform approach). This decision shapes the technology, the cost and the user experience of the whole project.

Native Android development targets Google's platform directly. The modern language is Kotlin, which has largely replaced the older Java, and the official development environment is Android Studio, built on the tools from JetBrains. User interfaces are increasingly built with Jetpack Compose, a declarative toolkit, alongside the traditional XML layouts. Android runs on an enormous range of devices from many manufacturers, which brings wide reach but also the challenge of fragmentation across many screen sizes and OS versions.

A central concept in Android is the Activity, which represents a single screen with which the user interacts, together with its lifecycle, a defined sequence of states such as created, started, resumed, paused and stopped. The system calls lifecycle methods as the app moves between the foreground and background, and the developer must handle these transitions correctly to save state, release resources and avoid leaks. Apps are distributed through the Google Play Store as APK or AAB packages.

Native iOS development targets Apple's devices and can only be done on a Mac using Apple's Xcode environment. The modern language is Swift, which has replaced the older Objective-C, and interfaces are built with the declarative SwiftUI framework or the older UIKit. Because Apple controls both the hardware and the software, iOS runs on a relatively small, uniform set of devices, which makes testing simpler but confines development to the Apple ecosystem and its tools.

The great advantage of the native approach, on either platform, is that the app has full and immediate access to every device capability and to the latest platform features, and it delivers the best possible performance and the most authentic look and feel. Its disadvantage is cost: building for both Android and iOS means writing and maintaining two separate code bases, often with two teams possessing different skills, effectively doubling much of the work.

Cross-platform development answers this by letting a single code base run on both platforms. The leading frameworks take different technical routes. React Native, from Meta, lets developers use JavaScript and React and renders real native interface components. Flutter, from Google, uses the Dart language and draws its own widgets on a high-performance canvas, giving consistent appearance and speed. Both aim to approach native quality while sharing most of the code.

Other cross-platform options exist for different backgrounds. .NET MAUI (the successor to Xamarin) lets C# developers target both platforms. Kotlin Multiplatform allows sharing business logic across Android, iOS and other targets while keeping native user interfaces. There are also web-based approaches, such as progressive web apps and frameworks like Ionic, which wrap web technologies in a native shell; these are the cheapest but usually the least capable and the least native-feeling.

The core trade-off of cross-platform development is between shared effort and platform fidelity. Writing once and running everywhere saves substantial time and cost and keeps behaviour consistent, but it can lag behind the newest native features, may not reach the absolute peak of performance, and sometimes requires platform-specific code anyway for device features or for matching each platform's design guidelines. The framework itself also adds a dependency that must be kept up to date.

The choice between native and cross-platform depends on the project's priorities. Native is preferable when maximum performance, deep hardware integration, or a perfectly platform-authentic experience is essential, or when the app targets only one platform. Cross-platform is preferable when time to market, budget and reaching both platforms quickly matter most, and when the app's requirements fall within what the framework supports well. Many successful products begin cross-platform and adopt native code selectively where it is needed.

Regardless of the approach, mobile apps share common concerns. They must manage a lifecycle of being interrupted by calls or backgrounded by the user, store data locally in a small database such as SQLite while synchronising with a server, request the user's permission to use sensitive features such as the camera or location, and adapt their layout responsively to many screen sizes. Efficient use of the network and the battery is a constant design consideration.

Distribution and platform rules are a distinctive part of mobile development. Apps reach users through official stores, Google Play and Apple's App Store, each with its own review process, guidelines and fees. Apple's review is notably strict, and both stores can reject apps that violate their policies. Updates are delivered through the store, and developers must support a range of OS versions, which affects how quickly new platform features can be relied upon.

Thus, mobile development is shaped by the constraints of resource-limited, touch-based devices and by the split between the Android and iOS platforms; developers choose between the native approach, which maximises performance and platform fidelity at the cost of maintaining two code bases, and cross-platform frameworks such as Flutter and React Native, which share most of the code across both platforms, selecting whichever best fits the project's performance needs, budget and time to market.
questions: What characteristics of mobile devices make mobile development different from desktop or server development?
What are the main languages and tools used for native Android and native iOS development?
What is the Activity lifecycle in Android, and why must a developer handle it correctly?
How do the cross-platform frameworks Flutter and React Native differ in their technical approach?
What are the main trade-offs between native and cross-platform development, and when is each preferable?

title: Creating machine-learning algorithms and integrating them into applications
outline: What machine learning is and its main types.
The machine-learning workflow from data to model.
Common algorithms and the role of neural networks.
Evaluating models and avoiding overfitting.
Integrating a trained model into a real application.
body: Machine learning (ML) is a branch of artificial intelligence in which a program improves its performance on a task by learning patterns from data, rather than following rules written explicitly by a programmer. Instead of coding every decision by hand, the developer supplies examples, and the learning algorithm builds a model that captures the underlying relationships. This approach excels precisely where explicit rules are impractical, such as recognising images, understanding language or predicting behaviour.

Machine learning is usually divided into three broad types. In supervised learning the training data is labelled, meaning each example comes with the correct answer, and the model learns to map inputs to outputs; this covers classification (predicting a category) and regression (predicting a number). In unsupervised learning the data has no labels, and the model finds structure by itself, for example by clustering similar items. In reinforcement learning an agent learns by trial and error, receiving rewards or penalties for its actions.

A model is not built in one step but through a workflow that begins long before any algorithm is chosen. The first and most time-consuming stages are collecting data, cleaning it of errors and missing values, and preparing features, the measurable input variables the model will use. The saying 'garbage in, garbage out' holds strongly here: the quality and relevance of the data usually matter more to the final result than the sophistication of the algorithm.

Feature engineering, the craft of selecting and transforming the input variables, is often decisive. Raw data must be turned into numeric features a model can use: text may be converted into word counts or embeddings, categories into numeric codes, and values scaled to comparable ranges. Good features make the patterns easy for the algorithm to find; poor or irrelevant features bury the signal in noise. Much of the practical skill of machine learning lies in this preparation.

A crucial discipline is splitting the available data into separate sets. The model is fitted on a training set, its settings are tuned on a validation set, and its final performance is judged on a test set that it has never seen.
